keywords: software-testing-quality-assurance software testing & qa comprehensive study materials for software testing & qa. unit4-pyqs unit 4: test planning & documentation (pyq solutions) unit 4: test planning & documentation (pyq solutions) solved pyqs pyq-solutions unit 4: test planning & documentation (pyq solutions) [!note] this unit focus on the ieee 829 test plan template and test case writing. a 10 mark case study is guaranteed from this unit. 1. case study: ieee 829 test plan [apr 2025 jan 2026 10 marks] application: cloud kitchen aggregator app / online learning platform. i) scope of testing includes functional testing of order management, menu updates, payment gateway integration, and user authentication. out of scope: backend infrastructure scaling and load testing (unless specified). ii) objectives verify that cloud kitchens can manage menus without errors. ensure real time order updates are received by customers. validate secure transactions via third party payment gateways. iii) risks delay in third party api responses (payment/delivery). data loss during high order volumes. security vulnerabilities in transaction modules. iv) strategy a combination of black box testing for ui/ux and integration testing for third party gateways. regression testing will be performed after every major bug fix. v) approach manual testing for usability and ui. automated testing for api integrations using tools like postman/selenium. 2. case study: writing test cases [apr 2025 10 marks] feature: online food ordering (cloud kitchen app). test case id description input data expected result : : : : tc01 verify adding item to cart select "paneer tikka" item added with correct price tc02 verify payment with valid card card details, otp transaction success, order placed tc03 verify order tracking active order id real time status shows "preparing" tc04 verify out of stock item select disabled item user should see "unavailable" alert 3. what is ieee 829? [short note] ieee 829 is a standard for software test documentation. it defines a set of documents for use in eight defined stages of software testing, each stage potentially producing its own separate type of document. the most common document is the software test plan (stp) . [!tip] exam hack: for case study questions, always use a table for test cases. it looks professional and is easier for the examiner to grade. use headers like test case id, description, steps, expected result.

title: Unit 5: Automation & Defect Management (PYQ Solutions)
excerpt: Unit 5: Automation & Defect Management (PYQ Solutions) [!NOTE] This unit covers the modern aspects of testing: Automation tools (Selenium, Postman, JIRA) and the Defect Life Cycle. A diagram of the Defect Life Cycle is a
keywords: software-testing-quality-assurance software testing & qa comprehensive study materials for software testing & qa. unit5-pyqs unit 5: automation & defect management (pyq solutions) unit 5: automation & defect management (pyq solutions) solved pyqs pyq-solutions unit 5: automation & defect management (pyq solutions) [!note] this unit covers the modern aspects of testing: automation tools (selenium, postman, jira) and the defect life cycle. a diagram of the defect life cycle is almost certain to be asked. 1. what is cast? explain its benefits. [apr 2025 5 marks] cast stands for computer aided software testing . it refers to the use of software tools to assist in various testing activities. benefits: speed: automation tools can execute tests much faster than humans. repeatability: tests can be run exactly the same way multiple times. reliability: eliminates human error (fatigue, oversight). coverage: can run thousands of complex test cases that are impossible manually. cost effective: long term reduction in testing costs for regression suites. 2. narrate the stages of defect life cycle. [apr 2025 jan 2026 5 10 marks] the defect life cycle (bug life cycle) is the specific journey of a defect from its discovery to its closure. stages: 1. new: when a defect is logged for the first time. 2. assigned: developer is assigned to fix it. 3. open: developer starts analyzing and fixing the bug. 4. fixed: developer has fixed the code and verified it. 5. pending retest: waiting for the tester to verify the fix. 6. retest: tester verifies the fix in the new environment. 7. verified/closed: tester confirms the fix is working. 8. reopened: if the fix fails during retest. 9. deferred: fix is postponed to future releases. 10. rejected: not a valid bug (e.g., duplicate or "works as designed"). diagram 3. explain the components of selenium. [jan 2026 5 marks] selenium is a suite of tools for automating web browsers. 1. selenium ide: a browser plugin for record and playback. 2. selenium webdriver: a programming interface to create and execute test cases. it interacts directly with the browser. 3. selenium grid: used for parallel testing across different browsers and operating systems. 4. testng: a testing framework (often used with selenium) that provides features like annotations, grouping, and reporting. 4. what are the steps to introduce a testing tool? [apr 2025 jan 2026 5 marks] 1. requirement analysis: identify the need for automation. 2. tool evaluation: compare available tools (open source vs. paid). 3. proof of concept (poc): run a small pilot project to check feasibility. 4. staff training: train the team on the selected tool. 5. implementation: start using the tool in actual projects. 6. maintenance & review: regularly update scripts and review the tool's roi. [!tip] exam hack: for the "defect life cycle" diagram, make sure to show the "reopened" and "deferred" states clearly. they are the most important transitions.

title: Question Bank
excerpt: STQA Exam Question Bank (Units 1 4) [!TIP] This question bank compiles solved exam questions for Units 1 to 4 based on your custom syllabus structure. Focus on bold terms, comparison tables, and equations. Unit 1: Softwa
keywords: software-testing-quality-assurance software testing & qa comprehensive study materials for software testing & qa. stqa-question-bank question bank question bank revision notes revision stqa exam question bank (units 1 4) [!tip] this question bank compiles solved exam questions for units 1 to 4 based on your custom syllabus structure. focus on bold terms, comparison tables, and equations. unit 1: software quality assurance fundamentals q1. define the following terms with suitable examples: quality, quality assurance, quality control. also differentiate between qa and qc. [5 marks] definitions 1. quality in software engineering, quality is the degree to which a software system, component, or process meets specified requirements and customer expectations. example : a banking application that processes transactions without errors, maintains data encryption, and responds within 2 seconds. 2. quality assurance (qa) quality assurance (qa) is a planned and systematic set of activities focused on the processes used to develop software. it is a preventative discipline. example : conducting developer code reviews and enforcing strict coding standard guidelines to prevent bugs from being written. 3. quality control (qc) quality control (qc) is the set of activities focused on identifying defects in the actual software product. it is a detection oriented and reactive discipline. example : running integration test suites or performing manual usability testing on a compiled software build. differentiating qa vs. qc dimension quality assurance (qa) quality control (qc) : : : focus process oriented (how the product is created). product oriented (checking the final output). approach proactive / preventive (aims to prevent bugs). reactive / corrective (aims to find and fix bugs). responsibility the entire development team and sqa staff. the testing team and code inspectors. example code standards, audits, design reviews. functional testing, system integration testing. q2. explain the major challenges faced in implementing software quality assurance in software development organizations. [5 marks] organizations face several hurdles when establishing a successful sqa culture: underestimation of qa budgets and timelines : quality planning, audits, and static inspections are often compressed or cancelled when project deadlines run tight. late qa involvement : sqa is frequently brought in late in the software development life cycle (sdlc), turning it into a reactive gate rather than a preventative process. developer resistance to standards : development teams may perceive strict coding rules, audits, and comprehensive documentation as bottlenecks to speed and creativity. dynamic and changing requirements : in agile environments, constantly shifting project scopes make it difficult to maintain stable quality baselines. lack of skilled resources : sqa requires professionals who understand process frameworks (like cmmi or iso) and automated testing tooling, which are often scarce. q3. discuss the role of sqa planning in software development. explain the importance of iso 9000 standards in maintaining software quality. [5 marks] role of sqa planning an sqa plan serves as the blueprint for quality activities throughout the software project. its role includes: defining quality standards : outlines the code style guidelines, metrics, and documentation standards to be used. scheduling audits and reviews : details exactly when and how software inspections, walkthroughs, and process audits will occur. allocating tools and resources : lists testing tools, verification environments, and assigns roles/responsibilities to team members. establishing defect tracking : specifies the workflow for logging, prioritizing, and closing defects. importance of iso 9000 standards iso 9000 is an international standard for quality management systems (qms). its significance includes: process consistency : enforces documented procedures for all development and testing phases, reducing variations. customer confidence : certifications demonstrate to clients that the company follows globally accepted quality standards. risk control : mandates regular internal audits and management reviews to detect and fix process loopholes. continuous improvement : requires teams to implement post project feedback loops to optimize workflows continuously. q4. explain various sqa activities performed during the software development life cycle (sdlc). [5 marks] sqa operates in parallel with the development lifecycle through the following activities: 1. requirements verification : reviewing software requirement specifications (srs) for ambiguities, contradictions, and testability. 2. design auditing : ensuring that architectural models conform to design standards and satisfy non functional performance requirements. 3. formal code reviews : participating in inspections and walkthroughs to catch code defects and security flaws before execution. 4. testing strategy oversight : verifying that the test plan covers all specifications and checking test execution coverage metrics. 5. process audits : evaluating whether development teams are adhering to organizational procedures (e.g., commit rules, release steps). 6. defect analysis : reviewing bug tracking databases to identify systemic quality trends and prevent recurring issues. q5. what are software quality factors? explain any five software quality factors with suitable examples. [5 marks] software quality factors quality factors represent the non functional attributes of a software product that determine its overall capability, utility, and user satisfaction. five key quality factors 1. correctness definition : the degree to which the software satisfies its specifications and fulfill the user's objectives. example : a payroll system calculating tax deductions exactly as per current government regulations. 2. reliability definition : the probability that software will run failure free for a specified duration in a specific environment. example : medical monitoring software that runs 24/7 without crashing or freezing. 3. efficiency definition : the amount of computer resources (cpu, memory, bandwidth) required by the software to perform its tasks. example : a mobile video streaming app that processes hd streams using minimal ram and battery power. 4. maintainability definition : the ease with which software can be updated, bug fixed, or refactored. example : a modular react application where changing the layout of the sidebar does not break the login flow. 5. portability definition : the ease with which the software can be moved from one hardware or operating system environment to another. example : a java program running seamlessly on windows, macos, and linux without code modifications. q6. define software reliability. explain the following reliability measurement factors with examples: rocof, mttf, mttr, mtbf, and pofod. [5 marks] software reliability software reliability is the probability of failure free software operation for a specified period of time in a specified environment. reliability measurement factors 1. rocof (rate of occurrence of failure) measures the frequency of failures observed over a given operational duration. example : a database engine experiencing $0.05$ failures per hour (meaning 1 failure expected every 20 hours). 2. mttf (mean time to failure) the average time the software runs before failing. used primarily for non repairable systems. example : a satellite control module designed to operate continuously for $10,000$ hours before a critical failure occurs. 3. mttr (mean time to repair) the average time required to troubleshoot, fix, and restore a failed system to an operational state. example : a web server crashing and taking an average of $15$ minutes for sysadmins to reboot and restore. 4. mtbf (mean time between failures) the average time between consecutive failures in repairable systems. formula : $\text{mtbf} = \text{mttf} + \text{mttr}$ example : if a server runs for $200$ hours (mttf) and takes $2$ hours to fix (mttr), the mtbf is $202$ hours. 5. pofod (probability of failure on demand) the probability that a system will fail to perform its required action when a service request (demand) is initiated. example : an emergency shutdown safety valve in a chemical plant having a pofod of $0.001$, meaning it might fail to shut down once in $1000$ emergency demands. unit 2: software testing fundamentals q1. define software testing. explain the objectives and importance of testing in software development. [5 marks] software testing definition software testing is the process of executing an application with the intent of finding defects and verifying that the software conforms to specified functional, performance, and security requirements. testing objectives finding defects : identifying software bugs, errors, and omissions before release. gaining confidence : providing stakeholders with verification that the system performs as expected. preventing defects : reviewing early requirement and design documents to prevent logical errors from entering the code. ensuring compliance : confirming that the system meets industry standards and regulatory compliance requirements. importance of testing financial savings : finding and fixing defects early in the sdlc is significantly cheaper than hotfixing bugs in production. product safety : testing ensures safety critical software (like medical devices or autopilots) does not cause physical harm. customer satisfaction : a bug free, fast, and easy to use application builds brand loyalty and user retention. high quality : enforces performance, security, compatibility, and reliability constraints on the software build. q2. differentiate between the following terms with suitable examples: error, bug, fault, defect, and failure. explain how software failures occur. [5 marks] key differences error / mistake : an incorrect human action made during programming or design. example : a developer writing < instead of <= in a loop check. bug / fault / defect : the representation of the error in the static source code or documentation. example : the loop exiting one index too early in the code. failure : the visible, runtime misbehavior of the software when executed. example : the application crashing or returning an incorrect calculation to the user. how software failures occur 1. a developer makes an error due to miscommunication, time pressure, or code complexity. 2. this error introduces a defect (bug) into the static code or configuration files. 3. during execution, the software runs along a specific path that triggers the defect. 4. once triggered, the defect corrupts the internal state of the program. 5. this corrupted state propagates to the user interface or output api, causing a visible failure . q3. explain the seven testing principles with practical examples. [5 marks] 1. testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence example : running $100$ tests on a portal and passing them all does not prove the system is $100\%$ bug free; it only proves that no bugs were found in those $100$ specific test paths. 2. exhaustive testing is impossible example : testing an input field that accepts $20$ character strings with all possible alphanumeric combinations is impossible. instead, testers prioritize using boundary values and equivalence classes. 3. early testing saves time and money example : reviewing requirement drafts at the start of a project to identify logical errors is far cheaper than fixing those same requirements after coding them. 4. defect clustering example : in an e commerce platform, $80\%$ of system bugs are often found clustered within a single complex module (like the payment gateway integration). 5. pesticide paradox example : running the same automated regression tests every week will eventually stop revealing new bugs. test cases must be updated regularly to find new issues. 6. testing is context dependent example : testing an online mobile game focuses on frame rates and usability, whereas testing banking transaction software focuses on data encryption, concurrency, and security. 7. absence of errors fallacy example : building a bug free portal is useless if it is hard to navigate, runs slowly, and does not meet the customer's actual business goals. q4. compare the v model and w model used in software testing. discuss their advantages and limitations. [5 marks] concept difference v model : pairs development activities on the left side (verification) with matching dynamic testing levels on the right side (validation). testing execution only starts after coding. w model : introduces parallel testing activities for every phase of development. testers work alongside developers to review requirements, review designs, and construct test cases before any code is compiled. comparison table attribute v model w model : : : testing start starts after the coding phase is completed. starts on day one during requirements gathering. activities primarily dynamic validation tests. equal focus on static verification (reviews) and validation. defect detection bugs are identified late during test execution. bugs are identified early in requirements/design reviews. cost to fix higher, as bugs are found late in the cycle. lower, as design flaws are caught before coding. advantages & limitations v model advantages : simple, structured, and easy to manage for small projects with stable requirements. limitations : testing starts late; high risk of expensive rework if requirements were misunderstood. w model advantages : early bug detection, higher test quality, and reduced development costs due to upfront verification. limitations : complex to coordinate; requires highly skilled testers and documentation from day one. q5. explain the following levels of testing with suitable examples: unit testing, integration testing, system testing, and user acceptance testing (uat). [5 marks] 1. unit testing description : testing the smallest isolation of code (e.g., individual functions, classes, or modules), typically written and executed by developers. example : testing a helper function calculateinterest(principal, rate, time) to verify it returns the correct mathematical value for valid inputs. 2. integration testing description : verifying that different modules, apis, or hardware interfaces interact and exchange data correctly. example : testing if the user registration module successfully stores user details in the mysql database and triggers a welcome email api. 3. system testing description : testing the completely integrated, end to end software application to evaluate its compliance with functional and non functional specifications. example : simulating a user searching for an item, adding it to the cart, checking out via credit card, and checking the receipt page. 4. user acceptance testing (uat) description : testing conducted by actual end users, clients, or product owners in a staging environment to confirm the application meets business requirements before production. example : a client walking through a custom inventory dashboard to sign off that the business reporting logic complies with their internal finance guidelines. q6. differentiate between the following: functional vs. non functional testing, smoke vs. sanity testing, and re testing vs. regression testing. [5 marks] 1. functional testing vs. non functional testing feature functional testing non functional testing : : : objective validates what the system does. validates how the system performs. focus user actions, inputs, and outputs. loading speeds, security, scalability. example testing if user login works with valid credentials. testing if the system handles $10,000$ concurrent users. 2. smoke testing vs. sanity testing feature smoke testing sanity testing : : : scope broad and shallow (tests overall build stability). narrow and deep (tests specific bug fixes). executed on initial/new software builds. relatively stable subsequent builds. example verifying if the app launches and the login page opens. verifying if the password reset link works after a hotfix. 3. re testing vs. regression testing feature re testing regression testing : : : goal confirms that a reported bug is fixed. confirms that changes have not broken existing features. execution runs the specific failed test case again. runs a suite of previously passed test cases. focus targeted on the bug fix area. covers unmodified parts of the application. unit 3: static & dynamic testing q1. explain the review process in static testing. differentiate between informal review, technical review, walkthrough, and inspection. [5 marks] review process the review process is a static testing technique where documents, specifications, or code are examined manually to detect defects and deviations from standards without running the application. differentiating review types review type formality led by primary objective key deliverables : : : : : informal review low peer (buddy check) quick feedback / brainstorming no formal notes walkthrough medium author (developer) knowledge sharing, training review report technical review medium technical lead / architect technical adequacy, design checks action list inspection high trained moderator strict defect detection using checklists defect metrics [!important] inspection is the most formal review technique, requiring pre defined entry/exit criteria and formal reporting of defect density metrics. q2. what is static analysis? explain the role of automated static analysis tools in software testing. [5 marks] static analysis static analysis is the process of evaluating software code structure, data flow, and configurations without executing the program. it focuses on finding syntax errors, security vulnerabilities, and design anomalies. role of automated static analysis tools automated tools scan massive codebases rapidly to identify issues that are tedious to find during manual code reviews: syntax and style violations : flags code style deviations (e.g., camelcase violations or missing semi colons). unreachable (dead) code detection : identifies declared variables that are never read, or lines of code after a return statement. security scanning : detects common security vulnerabilities like potential sql injection flaws or hardcoded secrets. complexity metrics : calculates cyclomatic complexity to flag functions that are too large and complex. resource leak detection : warns developers if file streams, database handles, or memory pointers are not closed properly. q3. explain the following black box testing techniques with suitable examples: equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, and decision table testing. [5 marks] 1. equivalence partitioning (ecp) ecp divides input data into classes that are expected to behave similarly. one value is selected from each partition. example : a discount code field accepts ages between 18 and 60 (inclusive). valid partition : [18 to 60] (e.g., input 30 $\rightarrow$ accepted) invalid partition 1 : < 18 (e.g., input 10 $\rightarrow$ rejected) invalid partition 2 : 60 (e.g., input 70 $\rightarrow$ rejected) 2. boundary value analysis (bva) bva tests values at the boundaries of equivalence partitions, as programming mistakes often occur at edge limits. example : using the same [18 to 60] age range, the boundary test cases are: minimum boundaries : 17 (rejected), 18 (accepted), 19 (accepted) maximum boundaries : 59 (accepted), 60 (accepted), 61 (rejected) 3. decision table testing used when combinations of inputs map to different actions. example : an online shopping site applies a discount if a user is a premium member and has a discount code : inputs & actions rule 1 rule 2 rule 3 rule 4 : : : : : : : : : is premium? yes yes no no has code? yes no yes no apply discount yes no no no q4. explain state transition testing with a suitable example. where is this testing technique useful? [5 marks] state transition testing state transition testing is a black box testing technique that evaluates how a system transitions between different states in response to inputs or events. example: atm account lock flow states : unlocked, 1 failure, 2 failures, locked. transitions : entering correct pin transitions the state back to unlocked. entering incorrect pin transitions unlocked $\rightarrow$ 1 failure $\rightarrow$ 2 failures $\rightarrow$ locked. where it is useful workflow applications : order tracking (placed $\rightarrow$ paid $\rightarrow$ shipped $\rightarrow$ delivered). ui navigation states : interactive wizard pages (step 1 $\rightarrow$ step 2 $\rightarrow$ complete). real time control systems : traffic light controllers, elevators, and vending machine software. q5. differentiate between the following white box testing techniques: statement coverage, branch coverage, and path coverage. [5 marks] these coverage metrics evaluate code execution completeness: metric focus strength complexity / effort : : : : statement coverage executes every individual line of code (statements) at least once. weak (may miss untested conditional outcomes). low effort. branch coverage executes every decision outcome (both true and false paths) at least once. stronger (includes statement coverage). medium effort. path coverage executes every possible execution path through the control flow graph. strongest (exhaustive execution coverage). extremely high (often impossible with loops). hierarchy $$statement\ coverage \subset branch\ coverage \subset path\ coverage$$ q6. what is cyclomatic complexity? explain mccabe’s cyclomatic complexity metric. [5 marks] cyclomatic complexity cyclomatic complexity is a software metric used to indicate the logical complexity of a program. it measures the number of linearly independent paths through the program's source code, which determines the minimum number of test cases required for branch coverage. mccabe’s formulas 1. predicate nodes formula $$v(g) = p + 1$$ where $p$ is the number of decision/predicate nodes (e.g. if, while, for statements). 2. edges and nodes formula $$v(g) = e n + 2$$ where $e$ = number of edges, $n$ = number of nodes in the program's control flow graph. example calculation decision nodes (p) : $1$ (if (x 10)) complexity : $v(g) = 1 + 1 = 2$ meaning exactly $2$ independent test cases are required to cover both decision branches. unit 4: test management q1. explain the roles and responsibilities of tester, test lead, and test manager in a software testing project. [5 marks] 1. tester responsibilities : writing test cases, preparing test data, and scripting test automation scenarios. executing manual test cases or running test scripts. logging defects in tracking tools (like jira) with logs and screenshots. performing re testing on fixed bugs to verify corrections. 2. test lead responsibilities : assigning daily testing tasks and coordinating schedule timelines. reviewing test design documents and verifying test cases created by testers. gathering and analyzing qa progress metrics (e.g., test execution status). acting as a link between developers and testers to clarify requirements. 3. test manager responsibilities : writing and approving the high level test strategy and test plan . managing project budgets, human resources, and timelines. selecting automation tools and infrastructure configurations. reporting overall product quality metrics directly to stakeholders and clients. q2. explain the components of a test plan as per ieee 829 standard test plan template. [5 marks] the ieee 829 standard outlines a structured blueprint to ensure all aspects of testing are documented: test plan identifier : a unique reference number to version and track the plan. introduction : general project overview and testing scope limits. test items : specific builds, code files, or modules to be tested. features to be tested : functional and non functional parameters included in the scope. features not to be tested : explicit out of scope exclusions (e.g., third party authentication apis). approach : the overall testing strategy (automation tools, manual testing types). item pass/fail criteria : the criteria that determine if a module has passed testing. suspension and resumption criteria : rules for stopping tests (e.g., if a blocking bug prevents execution) and when to restart. test deliverables : list of documents to be produced (test logs, defect reports, summaries). environmental needs : infrastructure requirements (hardware configuration, browser types, test databases). risks and contingencies : identifying potential bottlenecks and mitigation backup plans. approvals : signature sign off block for managers and clients. q3. what is test monitoring and test control? explain the role of test log, defect density, and test summary report in monitoring testing activities. [5 marks] test monitoring and test control test monitoring : the ongoing qa activity of collecting, analyzing, and reporting metrics from test execution to track progress. test control : taking corrective action based on monitoring data (e.g., allocating more testers to a delayed module) to keep testing aligned with the plan. roles in monitoring 1. test log a chronological record of test execution activities. role : provides audit proof of test execution steps, timestamps, tester names, and pass/fail details. 2. defect density a metric measuring the number of confirmed defects per software size unit (e.g., defects per kloc). formula : $$\text{defect density} = \frac{\text{number of confirmed defects}}{\text{size of software (lines of code / function points)}}$$ role : identifies which modules are highly prone to bugs and require restructuring or refactoring. 3. test summary report the final report prepared at the end of the testing cycle. role : details executed test results, passed/failed ratios, open bug status, and provides a release recommendation. q4. define test scenario, test suite, and test case. write suitable examples of positive and negative test cases. [5 marks] definitions test scenario : a high level description of a testable requirement. defines what to test. example : verify user payment functionality. test suite : a logical collection of related test cases grouped together for execution. example : regression test suite for checkout module. test case : a set of inputs, preconditions, execution steps, and expected outcomes. positive vs. negative test case examples scenario: login page validation (requires $6$ character alphanumeric password). positive test case tests system behavior using valid inputs. test case id : tc pos 01 input : password = p@ss12 (valid) expected result : user successfully logs in and accesses the dashboard. negative test case tests system error handling using invalid inputs. test case id : tc neg 01 input : password = 123 (too short) expected result : login fails; displays error message: "password must be at least 6 characters". q5. explain the importance of configuration management in software testing. [5 marks] configuration management (cm) tracks and controls changes, versions, and environments in software projects. its importance in testing includes: build version control : ensures testers execute test cases on the correct, target build version rather than outdated code. environment consistency : guarantees that configuration parameters (database schemas, configurations, api keys) are identical across development and testing environments. defect traceability : links logged defects to the exact version of the code and configuration settings that triggered them. preventing code conflicts : avoids issues where developer fixes overwrite or conflict with each other in the shared test build. rollback capabilities : allows the testing team to restore a previous stable version if a new build introduces critical blocking bugs. q6. explain the defect life cycle with a neat diagram. also discuss the importance of incident/defect reports in testing projects. [5 marks] defect life cycle diagram importance of defect reports a defect/incident report is a document containing detailed information about a discovered bug (including title, description, steps to reproduce, expected vs. actual outcomes, severity, and screenshots). its importance includes: developer communication : provides developers with the exact instructions and logs needed to reproduce and fix the bug without back and forth communication. defect tracking : serves as the primary source of truth for the status, history, and owner of a bug. quality metrics : supports calculating overall defect metrics, helping project managers evaluate software readiness.

title: PYQ APR 2025
excerpt: research methodology pyq apr 2025.md Research Methodology PYQ — APR 2025 Question 1 (a) Describe research process with suitable diagram in detail. [5] Answer Research Process Research process is a systematic sequence of
keywords: research-methodology research methodology comprehensive study materials for research methodology. research-methodology-pyq-apr-2025 pyq apr 2025 pyq apr 2025 solved pyqs pyq-solutions research methodology pyq apr 2025.md research methodology pyq — apr 2025 question 1 (a) describe research process with suitable diagram in detail. [5] answer research process research process is a systematic sequence of steps used to solve a research problem scientifically and effectively. steps in research process 1. identification of research problem select and define the problem clearly. determine scope and objectives. 2. literature review study books, journals, research papers, and previous work. understand existing knowledge and research gap. 3. formulation of research questions/objectives define purpose of research. prepare objectives and questions. 4. research design select suitable research design and methodology. decide tools and techniques. 5. data collection collect primary or secondary data. use surveys, interviews, questionnaires, etc. 6. sampling select representative sample from population. 7. data analysis analyze data using statistical methods. interpret findings. 8. conclusion summarize findings and results. 9. report writing prepare final research report systematically. diagram of research process important note: research process helps in conducting organized and reliable research. (b) explain importance of literature review in research. [5] answer importance of literature review literature review is the study of previous research work related to the selected topic. importance 1. provides background knowledge helps understand research topic deeply. 2. identifies research gap shows missing areas in previous studies. 3. avoids duplication prevents repetition of existing research. 4. helps in formulating objectives assists in preparing research questions and hypotheses. 5. improves research design guides selection of methods and techniques. 6. supports data interpretation helps compare current findings with previous research. 7. increases research quality makes research more reliable and valid. 8. provides theoretical support strengthens research using theories and concepts. literature review is essential for conducting meaningful and effective research. or (c) differentiate qualitative and quantitative research methods with suitable example. [5] answer difference between qualitative and quantitative research qualitative research quantitative research focuses on opinions and experiences focuses on numerical data uses non numeric data uses measurable numeric data small sample size large sample size flexible research approach structured research approach uses interviews and observations uses surveys and experiments gives detailed understanding gives measurable results analysis is descriptive analysis is statistical examples qualitative research interviewing students about online learning experience. quantitative research surveying students to calculate percentage using online learning apps. qualitative research explains “why”, while quantitative research explains “how much”. (d) what is research methodology? explain objectives of research. [5] answer research methodology research methodology is a systematic and scientific method used to conduct research, collect data, analyze information, and solve research problems. objectives of research 1. exploration to explore new ideas and facts. 2. description to describe characteristics of subjects or situations. 3. explanation to explain relationship between variables. 4. prediction to predict future outcomes based on research findings. 5. application to apply research findings for solving practical problems. 6. knowledge development to increase knowledge in a particular field. 7. problem solving helps solve real world issues systematically. research objectives provide proper direction to the study. question 2 (a) elaborate types of research with example. [10] answer types of research research is classified into different types based on purpose, method, and nature. 1. basic research also called pure research. conducted to increase theoretical knowledge. example research on ai algorithms. 2. applied research conducted to solve practical problems. example developing healthcare software. 3. qualitative research focuses on opinions and experiences. uses non numerical data. example interviewing students about online classes. 4. quantitative research uses numerical and measurable data. example surveying students for attendance statistics. 5. cross sectional research data collected at one point in time. example study of current internet usage. 6. longitudinal research conducted over long time period. example tracking student performance over years. 7. exploratory research used when little information is available. example exploring causes of low attendance. 8. descriptive research describes facts and characteristics. example customer satisfaction study. 9. experimental research studies cause effect relationship. example testing effect of teaching methods. 10. quasi experimental research similar to experimental research without complete control. example comparing existing classroom groups. or (b) what is hypothesis? [5] prepare a hypothesis (null & alternate) for following situations. answer hypothesis hypothesis is a tentative statement predicting relationship between variables. types of hypothesis 1. null hypothesis (h₀) states no relationship or effect exists. 2. alternate hypothesis (h₁) states relationship or effect exists. (i) social media usage on children’s behaviour null hypothesis (h₀) social media usage has no significant impact on children’s behaviour. alternate hypothesis (h₁) social media usage has significant impact on children’s behaviour. (ii) lectures attended and final exam scores null hypothesis (h₀) number of lectures attended has no effect on final exam scores. alternate hypothesis (h₁) number of lectures attended affects final exam scores. (c) what are the key components of a well structured research design? [5] answer components of research design research design is a blueprint for conducting research systematically. key components 1. research objectives defines goals of study. 2. hypothesis predicts relationship between variables. 3. variables includes independent and dependent variables. 4. data collection methods surveys, interviews, questionnaires, etc. 5. sampling design defines population and sample selection. 6. research methodology qualitative or quantitative approach. 7. validity ensures accuracy of research. 8. reliability ensures consistency of results. 9. data analysis techniques statistical tools for interpretation. 10. time and cost planning manages resources effectively. question 3 (a) explain probability and non probability sampling methods with suitable examples. [10] answer sampling sampling is the process of selecting a subset from a population for research study. 1. probability sampling in probability sampling, every member has equal chance of selection. types a) simple random sampling random selection using lottery method. example selecting 50 students randomly. b) systematic sampling select every kth element. example every 10th customer in a mall. c) stratified sampling population divided into groups. example students selected from different departments. d) cluster sampling population divided into clusters. example selecting one college from many colleges. advantages unbiased results reliable and accurate suitable for statistical analysis disadvantages time consuming costly requires population list 2. non probability sampling selection depends on convenience or judgment. types a) convenience sampling easily available participants selected. example surveying nearby students. b) judgment sampling participants selected based on expertise. example selecting experienced software developers. c) quota sampling fixed number selected from categories. example selecting equal male and female students. d) snowball sampling existing participants refer others. example research on cybercrime groups. advantages quick and simple low cost useful for exploratory research disadvantages high bias less reliable poor generalization or (b) suppose you are asked to select a sample for conducting a survey on students’ learning habits. which sampling method would be most suitable and why? what will be sample size for the same? [10] answer suitable sampling method stratified random sampling this method is suitable because students belong to different groups such as departments or years. reasons 1. ensures proper representation. 2. reduces bias. 3. provides accurate results. 4. useful for comparing groups. 5. improves reliability. sampling process 1. divide students into groups. 2. prepare student list. 3. select random samples from each group. 4. conduct survey. sample size suppose total population = 1000 students. suitable sample size: 10% to 20% of population. approximate sample size = 100 to 200 students. example group sample size first year 50 second year 50 third year 50 total sample size = 150 students data collection methods questionnaire interviews online forms question 4 (a) discuss chi square test. from the following data, find out whether there is any relationship between gender (male/female) & colour preferences: [10] colour males females total pink 10 40 50 black 70 30 100 yellow 30 20 50 total 110 90 200 (consider χ² value at 0.05 significance level and degree of freedom 2 = 5.99) answer chi square test chi square (χ²) test is a statistical test used to determine whether there is a significant relationship between two categorical variables. formula [ \chi^2 = \sum \frac{(o e)^2}{e} ] where: o = observed frequency e = expected frequency step 1: hypothesis formulation null hypothesis (h₀) there is no relationship between gender and colour preference. alternate hypothesis (h₁) there is relationship between gender and colour preference. step 2: expected frequencies formula [ e = \frac{(row\ total)(column\ total)}{grand\ total} ] expected frequency table colour male expected female expected pink 27.5 22.5 black 55 45 yellow 27.5 22.5 step 3: chi square calculation cell o e (o−e)²/e pink male 10 27.5 11.14 pink female 40 22.5 13.61 black male 70 55 4.09 black female 30 45 5.00 yellow male 30 27.5 0.23 yellow female 20 22.5 0.28 total χ² value [ \chi^2 = 34.35 ] step 4: decision calculated χ² value = 34.35 table χ² value = 5.99 since: [ 34.35 5.99 ] reject null hypothesis (h₀) conclusion there is significant relationship between gender and colour preference. or (b) what is t test? when is it used? [10] determine whether the average weight of sample of 20 mangoes is significantly different from population average weight of 70 gms. sample mean = 70.55 gm sample standard deviation = 2.82 gm (consider t value at 0.05 significance = 2.093) answer t test t test is a statistical test used to compare means and determine whether difference between sample mean and population mean is significant. uses of t test 1. used in hypothesis testing. 2. used when sample size is small. 3. compares sample and population means. 4. useful in research experiments. given data parameter value sample size (n) 20 population mean (μ) 70 sample mean (x̄) 70.55 standard deviation (s) 2.82 table t value 2.093 step 1: hypothesis null hypothesis (h₀) average weight of mangoes is equal to 70 gm. alternate hypothesis (h₁) average weight of mangoes is significantly different from 70 gm. step 2: formula [ t = \frac{\bar{x} \mu}{s/\sqrt{n}} ] step 3: calculation [ t = \frac{70.55 70}{2.82/\sqrt{20}} ] [ \sqrt{20} = 4.472 ] [ 2.82 / 4.472 = 0.63 ] [ 0.55 / 0.63 = 0.87 ] calculated t value [ t = 0.87 ] step 4: decision calculated t value = 0.87 table t value = 2.093 since: [ 0.87 < 2.093 ] accept null hypothesis (h₀) conclusion average weight of sample mangoes is not significantly different from population average weight of 70 gm. question 5 (a) select a research topic within a domain of your choice and prepare a research outline. [10] answer research topic “ai based chatbot for student support system” 1. title ai based chatbot for improving student support services 2. abstract this research focuses on development of ai based chatbot system for students. the chatbot provides instant responses related to admissions, exams, timetable, and academic support. 3. introduction ai is widely used in education systems. students require quick access to information. chatbots improve communication efficiency. 4. literature review 1. previous studies show ai improves student engagement. 2. chatbots reduce workload of staff. 3. existing systems provide automated support. 5. objectives 1. to design ai chatbot for students. 2. to improve student support services. 3. to reduce response time. 4. to analyze effectiveness of chatbot systems. 6. hypothesis null hypothesis (h₀) ai chatbot does not improve student support services. alternate hypothesis (h₁) ai chatbot improves student support services. 7. methodology component description research type applied research data collection surveys and feedback sampling method stratified sampling sample size 150 students tools python, nlp, machine learning 8. results and discussion 1. faster response time observed. 2. student satisfaction increased. 3. administrative workload reduced. 9. conclusion ai chatbots improve educational support systems. automated systems save time and resources. 10. references 1. research papers on ai 2. ieee journals 3. nlp and chatbot articles or (b) prepare an outline for research on “impact of social media on indian youth”. [10] answer title “impact of social media on indian youth” 1. introduction social media platforms are widely used by youth. social media affects education, behaviour, and communication. 2. objectives 1. to study social media usage among youth. 2. to analyze impact on education. 3. to study behavioural effects. 4. to identify positive and negative impacts. 3. hypothesis null hypothesis (h₀) social media has no significant impact on indian youth. alternate hypothesis (h₁) social media has significant impact on indian youth. 4. research gap 1. limited studies on indian students. 2. less research on mental health impact. 3. need for updated data. 5. data collection methods primary data questionnaire surveys interviews secondary data journals articles research papers 6. sampling component details sampling method stratified sampling target population indian youth sample size 200 students 7. results and discussion 1. excessive social media affects concentration. 2. educational content supports learning. 3. social media improves communication. 8. conclusion social media has both positive and negative effects. balanced use is necessary. awareness programs should be conducted. 9. references 1. social media research journals 2. government reports 3. research methodology books

title: PYQ JAN 2026
excerpt: research methodology pyq jan 2026.md Research Methodology PYQ — JAN 2026 Question 1 (a) Define Research Methodology and explain its significance in academic and professional contexts. [5] Answer Research Methodology Rese
keywords: research-methodology research methodology comprehensive study materials for research methodology. research-methodology-pyq-jan-2026 pyq jan 2026 pyq jan 2026 solved pyqs pyq-solutions research methodology pyq jan 2026.md research methodology pyq — jan 2026 question 1 (a) define research methodology and explain its significance in academic and professional contexts. [5] answer research methodology research methodology is a systematic and scientific approach used for identifying problems, collecting data, analyzing information, and drawing conclusions. it includes: research methods techniques procedures statistical tools used in research significance in academic context 1. helps students and researchers gain knowledge. 2. supports thesis, projects, and dissertations. 3. improves analytical and critical thinking. 4. encourages scientific and systematic study. 5. helps in discovering new concepts and theories. significance in professional context 1. helps organizations in decision making. 2. solves real world business and technical problems. 3. supports innovation and product development. 4. improves efficiency and productivity. 5. useful in fields like ai, cloud computing, cybersecurity, etc. research methodology ensures research is reliable, valid, and organized. (b) compare qualitative and quantitative research with suitable example. [5] answer difference between qualitative and quantitative research qualitative research quantitative research focuses on opinions and experiences focuses on numerical data uses non numeric data uses measurable data small sample size large sample size flexible approach structured approach uses interviews and observations uses surveys and experiments analysis is descriptive analysis is statistical explains “why” explains “how much” examples qualitative research interviewing students about online learning experience. quantitative research surveying students to calculate percentage using online learning apps. or (c) describe research process and steps explain with diagram. [5] answer research process research process is a sequence of systematic steps used to conduct research scientifically. steps in research process 1. identification of research problem 2. literature review 3. formulation of objectives 4. research design 5. data collection 6. sampling 7. data analysis 8. conclusion 9. report writing diagram of research process (d) explain common research methods used in computer applications, providing relevant examples. [5] answer common research methods in computer applications research in computer applications focuses on solving technical and practical problems. 1. experimental research tests systems, algorithms, or software. example comparing sorting algorithms. 2. survey research collects user feedback using questionnaires. example survey on mobile app usability. 3. case study method detailed study of specific organization or system. example cybersecurity implementation in a company. 4. simulation method creates virtual environment for testing. example network simulation in cloud computing. 5. comparative research compares technologies or techniques. example comparing machine learning algorithms. 6. prototype development develops working model for testing. example ai chatbot prototype. question 2 (a) elaborate types of research with example. [5] answer types of research 1. basic research conducted to increase theoretical knowledge. example research on artificial intelligence concepts. 2. applied research conducted to solve practical problems. example developing hospital management software. 3. qualitative research uses non numerical data. example student interviews about online classes. 4. quantitative research uses measurable numerical data. example survey on social media usage. 5. cross sectional research data collected at one point in time. example current internet usage study. 6. longitudinal research conducted over long time period. example tracking academic performance over years. (b) explain data collection methods with suitable example. [5] answer data collection methods data collection is the process of gathering information for research. 1. primary data collection data collected directly from original sources. methods a) survey method uses questionnaires and forms. example online student survey. b) interview method direct interaction with participants. example interviewing employees. c) observation method observing behaviour or activities. example observing customer buying patterns. d) experiment method conducting controlled experiments. example software performance testing. 2. secondary data collection data collected from existing sources. sources journals books research papers government reports example using census data for research. or (c) write note on ethics in research. [5] answer ethics in research research ethics are moral principles followed during research to ensure honesty and fairness. importance of ethics 1. maintains honesty in research. 2. protects participant privacy. 3. prevents misuse of data. 4. avoids plagiarism. 5. improves credibility of research. principles of research ethics 1. honesty results should be truthful. 2. confidentiality personal information should remain private. 3. informed consent participants must know purpose of research. 4. objectivity research should be unbiased. 5. respect for participants participants should be treated fairly. unethical practices copying without citation manipulating data false reporting (d) define research design with suitable diagram. [5] answer research design research design is a blueprint or plan used to conduct research systematically. importance of research design 1. provides proper direction. 2. reduces research errors. 3. saves time and cost. 4. improves reliability. components objectives hypothesis variables sampling design data collection methods diagram of research design question 3 (a) what is sampling discuss the process of sampling. calculate the sample size for a population of 100000. take confidence level as 95% and margin of error as 5%. [10] answer sampling sampling is the process of selecting a subset from a population for research study. importance of sampling 1. saves time and cost. 2. makes research manageable. 3. provides reliable data. 4. useful for large populations. process of sampling 1. define population 2. prepare sampling frame 3. select sampling method 4. determine sample size 5. select sample 6. collect data sampling process diagram sample size calculation formula [ n = \frac{n}{1 + n(e)^2} ] where: n = population size e = margin of error given data parameter value population (n) 100000 margin of error (e) 0.05 calculation [ n = \frac{100000}{1 + 100000(0.05)^2} ] [ 0.05^2 = 0.0025 ] [ 100000 \times 0.0025 = 250 ] [ 1 + 250 = 251 ] [ n = \frac{100000}{251} ] [ n \approx 398 ] final answer required sample size = 398 or (b) elaborate probability sampling techniques with advantages and disadvantages. [10] answer probability sampling probability sampling is a sampling method where every member has equal chance of selection. types of probability sampling 1. simple random sampling random selection method. example selecting students using lottery method. advantages unbiased simple disadvantages time consuming requires complete list 2. systematic sampling select every kth element. example every 10th customer. advantages easy to implement quick disadvantages pattern bias possible 3. stratified sampling population divided into groups. example students selected from departments. advantages better representation accurate results disadvantages complex process 4. cluster sampling population divided into clusters. example selecting one school from district. advantages cost effective useful for large populations disadvantages less accurate advantages of probability sampling 1. reliable results 2. less bias 3. better representation 4. suitable for statistical analysis disadvantages of probability sampling 1. expensive 2. time consuming 3. requires population list question 4 (a) discuss chi square test. from the data given below about treatment of 250 patients suffering from a disease, state whether the new treatment is superior to the conventional treatment. [10] treatment favourable non favourable total new 140 30 170 conventional 60 20 80 total 200 50 250 (consider χ² value at 0.05 significance level and degree of freedom 1 = 3.84) answer chi square test chi square test is a statistical method used to determine whether there is significant relationship between categorical variables. formula [ \chi^2 = \sum \frac{(o e)^2}{e} ] where: o = observed frequency e = expected frequency step 1: hypothesis formulation null hypothesis (h₀) there is no significant difference between new and conventional treatment. alternate hypothesis (h₁) new treatment is superior to conventional treatment. step 2: expected frequencies formula [ e = \frac{(row\ total)(column\ total)}{grand\ total} ] expected frequency table treatment favourable non favourable new 136 34 conventional 64 16 step 3: chi square calculation cell o e (o e)²/e new favourable 140 136 0.12 new non favourable 30 34 0.47 conventional favourable 60 64 0.25 conventional non favourable 20 16 1.00 total χ² value [ \chi^2 = 1.84 ] step 4: decision parameter value calculated χ² 1.84 table χ² 3.84 since: [ 1.84 < 3.84 ] accept null hypothesis (h₀) conclusion there is no significant evidence to prove that new treatment is superior to conventional treatment. or (b) two types of drugs were used on 6 and 5 patients for reducing weight. apply two sample t test. [10] drug a: 8, 10, 12, 9, 14, 13 drug b: 7, 9, 14, 12, 8 (consider t value at 0.05 significance = 2.262) answer two sample t test two sample t test is used to compare means of two independent samples. step 1: hypothesis null hypothesis (h₀) there is no significant difference in effectiveness of drug a and drug b. alternate hypothesis (h₁) there is significant difference in effectiveness of drug a and drug b. step 2: mean calculation drug a mean [ \bar{x 1} = \frac{8+10+12+9+14+13}{6} ] [ \bar{x 1} = 11 ] drug b mean [ \bar{x 2} = \frac{7+9+14+12+8}{5} ] [ \bar{x 2} = 10 ] step 3: variance values drug variance drug a 5.6 drug b 8.5 step 4: formula [ t = \frac{\bar{x 1} \bar{x 2}}{\sqrt{\frac{s 1^2}{n 1}+\frac{s 2^2}{n 2}}} ] step 5: calculation [ t = \frac{11 10}{\sqrt{\frac{5.6}{6}+\frac{8.5}{5}}} ] [ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.93+1.7}} ] [ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.63}} ] [ = \frac{1}{1.62} ] [ t = 0.62 ] step 6: decision parameter value calculated t value 0.62 table t value 2.262 since: [ 0.62 < 2.262 ] accept null hypothesis (h₀) conclusion there is no significant difference in effectiveness between drug a and drug b. question 5 (a) a mca student is writing his research report on “cybersecurity trends and threats”. suggest appropriate structure for finalizing research report. [10] answer structure of research report 1. title page should include: research title student name college/university name guide name submission date example title “cybersecurity trends and threats in modern systems” 2. abstract short summary of complete research. includes objectives, methodology, findings, and conclusion. 3. introduction should explain: meaning of cybersecurity need for cybersecurity current cyber threats importance of study 4. literature review review previous studies and journals. identify research gap. topics malware phishing ransomware ai security tools 5. research objectives 1. to study cybersecurity threats. 2. to analyze latest trends. 3. to understand impact of cyberattacks. 4. to suggest security measures. 6. hypothesis null hypothesis (h₀) cybersecurity techniques do not significantly reduce cyber threats. alternate hypothesis (h₁) cybersecurity techniques significantly reduce cyber threats. 7. research methodology component description research type applied research data collection surveys and reports sampling method random sampling sample size 100 participants 8. data analysis and interpretation use tables, charts, and graphs. apply statistical tools if required. 9. results and discussion possible findings: 1. increase in ransomware attacks. 2. ai improving cybersecurity. 3. lack of user awareness causing risks. 10. conclusion summarize important findings. suggest future improvements. 11. references research papers ieee journals books government reports 12. appendices questionnaire survey forms additional charts or (b) prepare an outline for research on topic “role of ai in education”. title: Unit 1: Introduction to Research (PYQ Solutions)
excerpt: Unit 1: Introduction to Research (PYQ Solutions) [!NOTE] This unit covers the definition, objectives, and process of research. It is the foundation for all other units. Expect questions on Qualitative vs. Quantitative an
keywords: research-methodology research methodology comprehensive study materials for research methodology. unit1-pyqs unit 1: introduction to research (pyq solutions) unit 1: introduction to research (pyq solutions) solved pyqs pyq-solutions unit 1: introduction to research (pyq solutions) [!note] this unit covers the definition, objectives, and process of research. it is the foundation for all other units. expect questions on qualitative vs. quantitative and the research process. 1. describe the research process with a suitable diagram. [apr 2025 jan 2026 10 marks] the research process consists of a series of actions or steps necessary to effectively carry out research. steps in research process: 1. defining the research problem: identifying the specific issue or question to be investigated. 2. reviewing the literature: studying existing research to understand what is already known. 3. formulating hypothesis: making a tentative assumption to test the research problem. 4. preparing research design: creating a blueprint for data collection and analysis. 5. data collection: gathering information using methods like surveys, interviews, or observations. 6. data analysis: processing and interpreting the collected data using statistical tools. 7. hypothesis testing: determining if the data supports or rejects the hypothesis. 8. generalization and interpretation: drawing conclusions from the findings. 9. reporting: writing the final research report or thesis. diagram 2. compare qualitative and quantitative research with suitable examples. [apr 2025 jan 2026 5 10 marks] basis qualitative research quantitative research : : : goal to understand underlying reasons/motives. to quantify data and generalize results. data non numerical (text, video, audio). numerical (statistics, percentages). approach subjective / inductive. objective / deductive. sample size small, non representative. large, representative. method interviews, case studies, focus groups. surveys, experiments, polls. example understanding why students skip lectures. measuring how many students skip lectures. 3. what is research methodology? explain its significance. [apr 2025 jan 2026 5 marks] definition research methodology is a way to systematically solve the research problem. it is a science of studying how research is done scientifically. significance: systematic approach: provides a logical sequence for conducting research. validity: ensures that the results obtained are valid and reliable. objectivity: helps the researcher remain unbiased during the study. efficiency: saves time and resources by providing a clear path. academic/professional growth: essential for masters/doctoral students to contribute to the field of knowledge. 4. explain the importance of literature review in research. [apr 2025 5 marks] 1. identifies research gap: helps find what has not been studied yet. 2. prevents duplication: ensures you are not repeating work already done. 3. provides context: helps understand the background and history of the topic. 4. refines methodology: learn from the successes and failures of previous researchers' methods. 5. supports findings: provides a basis for comparing your results with existing knowledge. [!tip] exam hack: for the "research process" question, always draw the flowchart (mermaid diagram above). it fetches full marks for clarity.

title: Unit 2: Research Problem & Hypothesis (PYQ Solutions)
excerpt: Unit 2: Research Problem & Hypothesis (PYQ Solutions) [!NOTE] This unit focus on the initial phases of research: defining the problem, formulating a hypothesis, and creating a research design. Expect numerical/case study
keywords: research-methodology research methodology comprehensive study materials for research methodology. unit2-pyqs unit 2: research problem & hypothesis (pyq solutions) unit 2: research problem & hypothesis (pyq solutions) solved pyqs pyq-solutions unit 2: research problem & hypothesis (pyq solutions) [!note] this unit focus on the initial phases of research: defining the problem, formulating a hypothesis, and creating a research design. expect numerical/case study questions on hypothesis formulation. 1. elaborate on types of research with examples. [apr 2025 jan 2026 10 marks] 1. descriptive research: describing a situation or phenomenon (e.g., census, survey of shopping habits). 2. analytical research: using facts or information already available to analyze and make evaluations (e.g., historical analysis). 3. applied research: finding a solution for an immediate problem (e.g., solving a decrease in sales for a specific company). 4. fundamental (basic) research: generalizing and formulating a theory (e.g., research in pure mathematics). 5. empirical research: based on experience or observation alone (e.g., testing the impact of a new drug). 2. what is a hypothesis? explain null and alternate hypothesis. [apr 2025 10 marks] a hypothesis is a tentative assumption made in order to draw out and test its logical or empirical consequences. types of hypothesis: null hypothesis ($h 0$): assumes there is no significant difference or relationship. it's what the researcher tries to disprove. alternate hypothesis ($h a$): assumes there is a significant difference or relationship. it's the researcher's actual prediction. 3. case study: formulate hypothesis [apr 2025 5 marks] situation: researcher wants to understand the impact of social media usage on children’s behavior. null hypothesis ($h 0$): there is no significant relationship between social media usage and changes in children's behavior. alternate hypothesis ($h a$): increased social media usage significantly leads to more aggressive/introverted behavior in children. 4. what are the key components of a research design? [apr 2025 jan 2026 5 marks] a research design is the conceptual structure within which research is conducted. 1. the sampling design: method of selecting items to be observed. 2. the observational design: conditions under which observations are created. 3. the statistical design: how the data will be analyzed. 4. the operational design: the procedures by which the sampling, observational, and statistical designs are carried out. [!tip] exam hack: for hypothesis formulation, always write both $h 0$ and $h a$ clearly. even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for both, providing both shows a deep understanding of the scientific method.

title: Unit 3: Sampling & Data Collection (PYQ Solutions)
excerpt: Unit 3: Sampling & Data Collection (PYQ Solutions) [!NOTE] This unit covers how we select participants and gather data. Expect questions on Sampling types (Probability vs. Non Probability) and Data Collection methods (Pr
keywords: research-methodology research methodology comprehensive study materials for research methodology. unit3-pyqs unit 3: sampling & data collection (pyq solutions) unit 3: sampling & data collection (pyq solutions) solved pyqs pyq-solutions unit 3: sampling & data collection (pyq solutions) [!note] this unit covers how we select participants and gather data. expect questions on sampling types (probability vs. non probability) and data collection methods (primary vs. secondary). 1. explain probability and non probability sampling methods. [apr 2025 10 marks] probability sampling (random) every member of the population has a known, non zero chance of being selected. 1. simple random sampling: every item has an equal chance (e.g., lottery method). 2. systematic sampling: selecting every $n^{th}$ item from a list. 3. stratified sampling: dividing population into groups (strata) and sampling from each. 4. cluster sampling: dividing population into clusters and selecting entire clusters randomly. non probability sampling (non random) selection is based on the researcher's judgment or convenience. 1. convenience sampling: selecting participants who are easy to reach. 2. purposive (judgmental) sampling: selecting based on specific characteristics. 3. quota sampling: selecting a specific number of people from different categories. 4. snowball sampling: existing participants recruit future participants. 2. explain data collection methods. [jan 2026 5 marks] 1. primary data collection: data collected for the first time by the researcher. observation: watching and recording behavior. interviews: face to face or telephonic conversations. questionnaires: written set of questions. 2. secondary data collection: data that has already been collected by someone else. internal sources: company records, reports. external sources: books, journals, websites, government reports. 3. numerical: calculate sample size [jan 2026 10 marks] scenario: population ($n$) = 100,000, confidence level = 95% ($z \approx 1.96$), margin of error ($e$) = 5% (0.05). using slovin's formula (simplified for exam purposes) or cochran's formula: for large populations, the sample size ($n$) is approximately: $n = \frac{z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1 p)}{e^2}$ assuming $p = 0.5$ (maximum variability): $n = \frac{1.96^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{0.05^2}$ $n = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.0025} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025} \approx 384$ result: a sample size of approximately 384 is required. 4. ethics in research [jan 2026 5 marks] 1. informed consent: participants must be informed about the research and agree to participate. 2. confidentiality: protecting the identity and data of the participants. 3. anonymity: not collecting identifying information if not necessary. 4. objectivity: avoiding bias in data collection and interpretation. 5. plagiarism: giving proper credit to others' work and avoiding copying. [!tip] exam hack: if a question asks for a "sampling method for students' learning habits," the best answer is stratified random sampling (grouping by year/course) to ensure representation from all segments.

excerpt: Unit 4: Data Analysis & Statistical Testing (PYQ Solutions) [!NOTE] This unit covers statistical tests like Chi Square, T Test, and ANOVA. Expect at least one 10 mark numerical on Chi Square or T Test. 1. What is Chi Squ
keywords: research-methodology research methodology comprehensive study materials for research methodology. unit4-pyqs unit 4: data analysis & statistical testing (pyq solutions) unit 4: data analysis & statistical testing (pyq solutions) solved pyqs pyq-solutions unit 4: data analysis & statistical testing (pyq solutions) [!note] this unit covers statistical tests like chi square, t test, and anova. expect at least one 10 mark numerical on chi square or t test. 1. what is chi square ($\chi^2$) test? [apr 2025 jan 2026 5 marks] chi square test is a non parametric test used to determine if there is a significant association between two categorical variables. formula: $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(o e)^2}{e}$ where: $o$ = observed frequency $e$ = expected frequency 2. numerical: chi square test [apr 2025 10 marks] problem: relationship between gender and colour preference. gender pink black yellow total : : : : : male 10 70 30 110 female 40 30 20 90 total 50 100 50 200 step 1: null hypothesis ($h 0$) there is no significant relationship between gender and colour preference. step 2: calculate expected frequencies ($e$) $e = \frac{(\text{row total} \times \text{column total})}{\text{grand total}}$ $e(\text{male, pink}) = (110 \times 50) / 200 = 27.5$ $e(\text{male, black}) = (110 \times 100) / 200 = 55$ $e(\text{male, yellow}) = (110 \times 50) / 200 = 27.5$ $e(\text{female, pink}) = (90 \times 50) / 200 = 22.5$ $e(\text{female, black}) = (90 \times 100) / 200 = 45$ $e(\text{female, yellow}) = (90 \times 50) / 200 = 22.5$ step 3: calculate $\chi^2$ $\chi^2 = \frac{(10 27.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(70 55)^2}{55} + \dots$ $\chi^2 = 11.13 + 4.09 + 0.22 + 13.61 + 5.0 + 0.27 = \mathbf{34.32}$ step 4: conclusion table value at $\alpha=0.05, df=2$ is 5.99 . since calculated $\chi^2$ (34.32) 5.99, we reject $h 0$ . result: gender significantly influences colour preference. 3. what is t test? when is it used? [apr 2025 10 marks] a t test is a parametric test used to compare the means of two groups. it is used when: the population standard deviation is unknown. the sample size is small ($n < 30$). types: 1. one sample t test: compare sample mean with population mean. 2. independent two sample t test: compare means of two independent groups. 3. paired t test: compare means from the same group at different times. [!tip] exam hack: for chi square, always check the degree of freedom ($df$). formula: $df = (r 1)(c 1)$ for a $2 \times 3$ table: $df = (2 1)(3 1) = 2$.

[apr 2025 jan 2026 10 marks] topic example: role of ai in education / impact of social media. standard structure: 1. title: clear, concise, and descriptive. 2. abstract: a 200 300 word summary of the entire study. 3. introduction: background of the study, problem statement, and significance. 4. literature review: summary of existing work and identifying the research gap. 5. objectives: what the research aims to achieve. 6. hypothesis: predictable statements to be tested ($h 0$ and $h a$). 7. methodology: research design, sampling, and data collection methods. 8. results & discussion: presentation of findings and their interpretation. 9. conclusion: summary of findings, limitations, and future scope. 10. references: list of all sources cited (apa/mla style). 2. case study: outline for "impact of social media on indian youth" [apr 2025 10 marks] title: a study on the psychological impact of instagram usage on indian undergraduates. objectives: 1. to measure the average time spent on social media daily. 2. to analyze the correlation between social media usage and academic performance. hypothesis: $h 0$: there is no significant impact of social media on academic scores. $h a$: high social media usage significantly reduces academic concentration. methodology: sampling: stratified random sampling (10 colleges across india). data collection: online questionnaire (google forms). research gap: lack of longitudinal studies on indian rural youth's social media habits. 3. what is a research report? [short note] a research report is a document that presents the findings of a research project. it is the final stage of the research process and serves as a formal record of the study. it must be written in an objective, clear, and professional language. [!tip] exam hack: when asked to select a research topic, choose something "safe" and "modern" like "cybersecurity challenges in cloud computing" or "machine learning for early disease detection." these topics have clear objectives and methodologies.
